cells.label: The Web
cells.description: Web crawl data is the dominant ingredient in most large language models — CommonCrawl alone processes roughly 3 billion URLs per month. This category covers everything publicly indexable: news sites, personal blogs, corporate pages, how-to guides, recipe sites, opinion columns, and the vast middle of the internet that is neither expertly written nor entirely disposable.
bullets: CommonCrawl: petabyte-scale monthly web snapshots going back to 2008
Quality filters (C4, Gopher, OSCAR) discard ~90% of raw crawl data as spam, boilerplate, or near-duplicate
Web text introduces enormous vocabulary diversity but also misinformation, SEO gibberish, and temporal bias

cells.label: Code & Math
cells.description: Structured, formal, verifiable knowledge. Code has unambiguous semantics — it either runs or it doesn't. Mathematics has proofs. This is where models learn to reason precisely, to work through constraint satisfaction, and to follow chains of implication without drifting. The overrepresentation of code relative to its internet share is intentional: it punches above its weight in teaching formal thinking.
bullets: GitHub alone hosts 150M+ public repositories across hundreds of programming languages
Mathematical LaTeX source preserves symbolic structure that PDF rendering loses
Code is among the most deduplicated data — boilerplate and generated code is aggressively filtered

cells.label: Academic Papers
cells.description: Scholarly writing from peer-reviewed journals and preprint servers. Academic papers have a distinctive structure — abstract, methods, results, discussion, references — that teaches precise argumentation and hedged claims ('we hypothesize,' 'the data suggest'). The corpus includes everything from computer science to biomedicine to economics.
bullets: PubMed Central: 7M+ open-access biomedical full texts
arXiv: 2M+ preprints across STEM disciplines
Citation networks teach models the relationship between claims and evidence

cells.label: Books
cells.description: Long-form human thought — the most densely argued, most carefully edited form of written knowledge. Books teach models sustained reasoning across hundreds of pages, narrative coherence, chapter structure, and the thousand-year accumulation of ideas that constitute Western (and increasingly global) intellectual heritage. The legal status of this category is contested.
bullets: Project Gutenberg: 70,000+ public-domain works — everything before 1928
Books3 / LibGen derivatives: vast collections of copyrighted books scraped without authorization
Long-context coherence: books are the primary training signal for multi-paragraph reasoning

cells.label: Forums & Q&A
cells.description: Informal discussion and community knowledge — the internet arguing, explaining, commiserating, and helping. Reddit, StackExchange, Hacker News. The tone ranges from thoughtful explanation to flame war, but at its best this category captures how humans actually reason through problems collaboratively and in real time.
bullets: Reddit: 800M+ posts and comments across 130,000+ active subreddits
StackExchange: 200+ Q&A sites beyond Stack Overflow, covering every domain
The 'explain like I'm five' register — lay explanations of complex topics — comes from here

cells.label: Wikipedia
cells.description: The encyclopedia that trained almost every language model that exists. Wikipedia is written in a distinctive neutral, encyclopedic register — 'X was born in Y and is known for Z' — that has influenced the default voice of AI more than any other single source. It is also available in 300+ languages.
bullets: English Wikipedia: 6.7M articles, 4.4B words — heavily upweighted in most training runs
Available in 300+ languages, making it the primary signal for multilingual models
The 'Wikipedia voice' — neutral, definitional, present-tense — is now the default AI register

cells.label: Legal & Government
cells.description: Court opinions, statutes, regulations, patents, and government documents. Legal writing has a distinctive structure: citations, precedent, numbered provisions, 'whereas' clauses, and very careful attention to the exact meaning of words. Government documents include everything from tax forms to Congressional records to administrative agency rulemakings.
bullets: FreeLaw / CourtListener: 7M+ federal and state court opinions
US Patent and Trademark Office: 11M+ patents going back to 1836
Federal Register: 85+ years of regulatory rulemaking in formal prose

cells.label: Multilingual
cells.description: Parallel corpora and multilingual text collections — documents that exist in multiple languages simultaneously. These are essential for training multilingual models because they provide alignment signal: the same meaning expressed in different linguistic forms. European Union documents are among the largest high-quality parallel corpora in the world.
bullets: Europarl: EU Parliament proceedings in 24 official languages, aligned sentence-by-sentence
CC-100: CommonCrawl filtered for 100 languages, unevenly quality-controlled
OPUS: aggregation of 90+ parallel translation corpora

cells.label: Transcripts
cells.description: Written records of spoken language — the closest training data gets to the oral register that humans default to in conversation. YouTube auto-captions, professional subtitles, podcast transcripts. This data teaches models how people actually talk: contractions, incomplete sentences, disfluencies, colloquialisms.
bullets: YouTube Data API: auto-generated captions for hundreds of millions of videos
OpenSubtitles: 3.7M subtitle files across 60+ languages, sourced from film and TV
Spoken language is structurally different from written — simpler syntax, more deixis ('this,' 'here,' 'now')

cells.label: Synthetic Data
cells.description: Text generated by AI models to train other (or future versions of the same) AI models. Synthetic data is used primarily for pre-training augmentation in areas where human-generated data is scarce — especially formal mathematics and structured reasoning. Its use in pretraining is recent (2023+) and growing, though it constitutes a small share of current corpora.
bullets: rStar-Math: 747K math problems with step-by-step reasoning, generated by LLMs for LLM training
Phi-1, Phi-2, Phi-3 (Microsoft): deliberately trained on AI-generated 'textbook quality' text
Risk of 'model collapse': models trained on synthetic data derived from themselves may degrade over generations

cells.label: Unknown
cells.description: The part they don't tell you about. Most frontier model training runs include proprietary, licensed, or otherwise undisclosed data. The Foundation Model Transparency Index found that in 2025, the average transparency score for training data was 40 out of 100 — meaning most companies don't fully disclose what they trained on.
bullets: FMTI 2025: average transparency score 40/100 for training data disclosure
OpenAI, Google, Anthropic: none have fully disclosed their training data composition
Licensing deals with news publishers, content aggregators, and data brokers — amounts undisclosed
